title: Introductions
subtitle: Name, origin, and the pronouns я / ты / вы

vocabulary.meaning: my name is (literally: they call me)
glad / pleased (said by a male speaker)
glad / pleased (said by a female speaker)

hook.text: You can greet someone in Russian. Now what? In any conversation, the next move is exchanging names and finding common ground. This lesson gives you the exact phrases for a complete introduction — from 'hello' to 'nice to meet you' — in both formal and informal registers.

why.text: Russian has no verb 'to be' in the present tense — 'I am a student' is literally 'I student' (Я студент). This seems strange but it's actually simpler: you don't have to conjugate anything. The flipside is that Russian has formal/informal pronouns (вы/ты) that affect all the verbs around them. Getting these right from the start prevents deeply ingrained errors.

cards.title: The pronouns я, ты, вы
cards.body: Russian distinguishes formal and informal 'you' — just like French tu/vous or German du/Sie. ты is singular informal (friends, family). вы is formal singular OR any plural. Use вы until someone invites you to use ты.

cards.title: My name is... — меня зовут
cards.body: The literal translation is 'they call me' — меня (me, accusative) зовут (they call). There's no word for 'my' or 'is' here. Don't try to translate it word for word — learn the phrase as a unit.

cards.title: Where are you from — откуда + из
cards.body: To say where you're from, use я из + city/country name. The city name may take a genitive ending — for now, just learn the most common ones as phrases. откуда (from where) is the question word.

cards.title: Nice to meet you
cards.body: рад/рада познакомиться is 'pleased to meet you'. The key: рад is used by male speakers, рада by female speakers. This is your first encounter with grammatical gender affecting the speaker — not just the noun. Russian adjectives agree with the subject, and the subject's biological gender determines which form to use when talking about yourself.

examples.meaning: Very pleasant / nice to meet you (gender-neutral alternative)

questions.prompt: How do you say 'my name is...' in Russian?
questions.explanation: меня зовут (menya zovut) = my name is. Literally 'they call me'.

questions.prompt: You are female. How do you say 'pleased to meet you'?
questions.explanation: рада is the feminine form of рад. Female speakers use рада; male speakers use рад.

questions.prompt: What is the formal way to ask 'what is your name?'
questions.explanation: Как вас зовут? (Kak vas zovut?) — formal. вас = you (formal, genitive). Informal version: Как тебя зовут?

cards.title: No verb 'to be' in present tense
cards.body: Russian drops the present tense of 'to be' entirely. There is no equivalent of 'am', 'is', 'are' in a Russian present tense sentence. Я студент = 'I student' = I am a student. Это книга = 'This book' = This is a book. This applies throughout the language — not just in introductions.

cards.title: Switching from вы to ты
cards.body: In Russian culture, switching from formal вы to informal ты is a social milestone — it's called перейти на ты (to switch to ты). You don't do it unilaterally; you wait for the other person to invite it, or one person says 'Можно на ты?' (May I use ты with you?). Skipping this step with the wrong person is rude.

examples.meaning: Can we use ты? (switching to informal)

questions.explanation: я (ya) = I. Always lowercase in Russian (unlike English 'I').

questions.prompt: How do you ask 'where are you from?' formally?
questions.explanation: Откуда вы? (Otkuda vy?) = Where are you from? (formal). Informal: Откуда ты?
вы = you (formal singular) OR you (plural). Use with strangers, elders, and multiple people.
Меня зовут Антон. = My name is Anton. The phrase is меня зовут.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I am from Russia'?
questions.explanation: Я из России. (Ya iz Rossii.) = I am from Russia. из = from, России = Russia (genitive).

questions.prompt: What does как mean in Как вас зовут?
questions.explanation: как = how / what. Как вас зовут? = What is your name? (literally 'how do they call you?')

questions.prompt: You're male. You meet someone new. How do you say 'pleased to meet you'?
questions.explanation: Рад познакомиться. (Rad poznakomit'sya.) = Pleased to meet you (male speaker). Female speakers say: Рада познакомиться.

rules: я = I, ты = you (informal), вы = you (formal / plural)
меня зовут = my name is (literally: they call me)
я из + country/city = I am from...
рад (male) / рада (female) познакомиться = pleased to meet you
No present-tense 'to be': Я студент = I am a student
summary.nextLesson: Next: numbers 1–10 — the foundation for dates, times, prices, and counting.

questions.prompt: You are female. How do you say 'pleased to meet you'?
How do you say 'where are you from?' (formal)?
